title: PyEntrez
description: Python wrapper for NCBI's EDirect command-line tools. Provides a programmatic API for searching PubMed and other NCBI databases, fetching records, discovering cross-database links, pulling citation counts, and querying by MeSH terms. Includes batch processing with parallel workers for bulk operations.

blog.subtitle: The wrapper I wrote because I got tired of shell pipes

sections.heading: EDirect is great and also bad
paragraphs: NCBI ships EDirect, a set of command line tools that let you query PubMed and the rest of their databases. It is the gold standard and it is also a pipeline of shell commands that you chain with pipes and that break in silence if you do anything remotely concurrent.
I wanted a Python API. PyEntrez is that. A thin wrapper around esearch, efetch, elink, and einfo that returns real objects and fails loudly.

sections.heading: What it does out of the box
paragraphs: Search PubMed with a plain string or a structured MeSH query. Fetch full records. Follow cross-database links, for example from a gene to the papers that discuss it. Pull citation counts.
All of it is typed. All of it can be parallelized with a batch worker that respects NCBI rate limits, because NCBI will rate limit you and it is embarrassing to get a letter about it.

sections.heading: Why it stayed small
paragraphs: I kept rejecting features. A library that tries to be a search engine on top of an API is a library that will break every time the API changes. PyEntrez only exposes what EDirect exposes. If you want something fancier, build it on top.
The core stays boring and boring is why it still works.
